127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). 5502	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
5503	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
5504	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
5505	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
5506	  \[
5507	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
5508	  \]
5509	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
5510	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
5511	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
5512	\end{lemma}
5513	
5514	\begin{proof}
5515	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5516	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
5517	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
5518	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
5519	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
5520	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
5521	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
5522	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
5523	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
5524	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
5525	
5526	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
5527	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
5528	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
5529	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
5530	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
5531	  \[
5532	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
5533	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
5534	  \]
5535	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
5536	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
5537	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
5538	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation; it factors into \emph{two}
5539	  legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled
5540	  over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
5541	
5542	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
5543	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
5544	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
5545	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain
5546	  \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) to the
5547	  restricted domain \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV) \subseteq
5548	  \mathrm{Opens}\,X\), and hence identifies the two Hom-modules computed over these matched
5549	  slices. This is a genuine slice-site Hom base-change --- a Beck--Chevalley square on the
5550	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set
5551	  of \(fV\) --- \emph{not} a fibrewise identity.
5552	
5553	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
5554	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
5555	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
5556	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
5557	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
5558	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
5559	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
5560	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
5561	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
5562	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}).
5563	
5564	  The Step-4 isomorphism is the sectionwise composite of leg (A) followed by leg (B),
5565	  packaged over the slice with poset-thin naturality: the slice-reindexing coherence is
5566	  \emph{light}, because \(\mathrm{Opens}\) is a thin poset and the relevant squares commute
5567	  by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\), whereas legs (A) and (B) carry the substantive content.
5568	
5569	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
5570	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For
5571	  the tensor product the transport \emph{collapsed to leg (B) alone}: because \(\otimes\)
5572	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single
5573	  ground-ring tensor equivalence, with no domain slice left to re-index. The dual's
5574	  non-sectionwise nature removes that collapse and forces the additional leg (A): the
5575	  domain \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) genuinely lives over a varying slice and must be
5576	  transported before the ground ring can be reconciled.
5577	
5578	  We caution that the naive ``fixed-value-category slice equivalence'' of
5579	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} is \emph{not} applicable to leg (A). That instrument
5580	  operates in a fixed value category over a fixed base ring, whereas the residual of
5581	  leg (A) lives at the \emph{presheaf} level, with a per-section ring \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)
5582	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
5583	  leg (A) must therefore be built directly at the presheaf-of-modules / varying-ring level.
5584	
5585	  Composing legs (A) and (B) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
5586	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values; assembling these into a
5587	  presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism and sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of
5588	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} yields the stated isomorphism
5589	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
5590	
5591	  \emph{An alternative route}, not committed to here, derives the isomorphism from the
5592	  already-established \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} by uniqueness of monoidal inverses:
5593	  the dual is the \(\otimes\)-inverse of an invertible module and restriction commutes with
5594	  \(\otimes\), so evaluation/coevaluation naturality transports the inverse. This sidesteps
5595	  leg (A) entirely, and is a candidate should the inverse-uniqueness glue prove cheaper than
5596	  building the slice-site base-change directly.
5597	\end{proof}

5627	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).

5811	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}
5812	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homOfLocalCompat}
5813	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and \(\{U_i\}\) an
5814	  open cover of \(X\). Suppose given, for each \(i\), a morphism
5815	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that
5816	  for all \(i, j\) the restrictions of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree.
5817	  Then there is a unique global morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
5818	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\).
5819	\end{lemma}
5820	
5821	\begin{proof}
5822	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_homMk, lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
5823	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
5824	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
5825	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.
5826	
5827	  \emph{(i) Glue the underlying ab-sheaf morphism.} Forget \(M\) and \(N\) to their
5828	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
5829	  \(\mathcal{H}\) (\(\mathtt{presheafHom}\) of the underlying presheaves of abelian groups
5830	  of \(M\) and \(N\)), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
5831	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
5832	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
5833	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
5834	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
5835	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
5836	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
5837	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
5838	  strictly more bookkeeping.
5839	
5840	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}
5841	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}(U_i)\)
5842	  of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_hom_local_section},
5843	  manufactured from \(f_i\). Its component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given
5844	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
5845	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
5846	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
5847	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole
5848	  construction is concentrated in the \emph{naturality condition} of
5849	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
5850	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
5851	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece to be
5852	  built and verified first, as a standalone lemma.
5853	
5854	  This \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality is precisely the \emph{section-direction
5855	  slice} of the comparison packaged in \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}:
5856	  converting the local datum \(f_i\), which lives over the open-immersion pullback
5857	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U_i}\), into a section of \(\mathcal{H}\) indexed over the
5858	  slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\) is the one-sided realisation of the open-immersion
5859	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport. As there, the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation is
5860	  controlled by the down-set identity \(\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}(V)) = V\) for
5861	  \(V \subseteq U_i\), and the coherence is automatic on the thin poset
5862	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). This A-engine uses the
5863	  sheaf-site equivalence \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}; the C-bridge
5864	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, by contrast, uses a per-open slice comparison at
5865	  the presheaf level over the \emph{varying} section ring \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), and so
5866	  does \emph{not} reduce to the same sheaf-site root --- the two are independent and
5867	  must be built separately.
5868	
5869	  Of the remaining four sub-steps, three are mechanical and one --- the cocycle bridge
5870	  (a) --- carries the genuine content. We spell it out.
5871	
5872	  \emph{(a) The cocycle/\(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition on the family
5873	  \((\mathtt{localSection}\,i)_i\).} The condition \(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) asks that for
5874	  every pair of indices \(i, j\) the two restrictions of \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\) and
5875	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,j\) to the overlap coincide as sections of the hom-sheaf
5876	  \(\mathcal{H}\) over \(U_i \sqcap U_j\), i.e.\ an honest equality in the single abelian
5877	  group \(\mathcal{H}(U_i \sqcap U_j)\). We must therefore identify the overlap-agreement
5878	  hypothesis on the family \((f_i)_i\) that furnishes this. The correct hypothesis --- and
5879	  the only one a caller can actually produce --- is the \emph{sectionwise} one:
5880	
5881	  \begin{quote}
5882	    for all indices \(i, j\) and every open \(V\) with \(V \subseteq U_i\) and
5883	    \(V \subseteq U_j\), the two section maps \((f_i).\mathtt{app}(V)\) and
5884	    \((f_j).\mathtt{app}(V)\) agree as morphisms in the single, fixed abelian-group hom-type
5885	    \(M(V) \to N(V)\), the value of the hom-presheaf \(\mathcal{H} = \mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) at
5886	    \(V\).
5887	  \end{quote}
5888	
5889	  This is exactly the data a caller has at hand: two local morphisms that literally agree on
5890	  every open contained in both \(U_i\) and \(U_j\).
5891	
5892	  It is worth contrasting this with a naive alternative that does \emph{not} work. One might
5893	  try to compare the two double-restrictions \((f_i)|_{U_i \cap U_j}\) and
5894	  \((f_j)|_{U_i \cap U_j}\) directly, as morphisms reached by the two slice-restriction
5895	  routes. But these two morphisms do not live in a common type at all: each is the image of
5896	  \(f\) under the pullback-of-modules functor along a \emph{different} slice-restriction
5897	  map, and the resulting source and target objects are sheafifications of pullback presheaves
5898	  along different morphisms. Such objects are only \emph{isomorphic}, through a nontrivial
5899	  comparison isomorphism, and are \emph{not} propositionally equal. A heterogeneous
5900	  comparison of the two double-restrictions therefore cannot be reduced to an honest
5901	  equation --- there is no object equality to transport along, so no
5902	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) of the thin poset and no heterogeneous-to-homogeneous
5903	  elimination applies --- and, crucially, no caller can produce such a datum in the first
5904	  place. The sectionwise hypothesis sidesteps this entirely by comparing only the section
5905	  maps, which live in the fixed group \(M(V) \to N(V)\).
5906	
5907	  With the sectionwise hypothesis in hand the passage to \(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) is
5908	  \emph{direct}. Restrict \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\) to the overlap \(U_i \sqcap U_j\). By
5909	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_hom_local_section}, the component of \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\)
5910	  at an open \(V \subseteq U_i\) is precisely \((f_i).\mathtt{app}(V')\), where
5911	  \(V' = \iota_i^{-1}(V)\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
5912	  arising from the down-set identity \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\); restricting
5913	  to the overlap simply evaluates this component at the opens \(V \subseteq U_i \sqcap U_j\),
5914	  and likewise for \(j\). Thus both restrictions, evaluated at such a \(V\), are conjugates
5915	  of \((f_i).\mathtt{app}(V')\) and \((f_j).\mathtt{app}(V')\) by \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)
5916	  morphisms, all lying in the fixed group \(M(V) \to N(V)\). The sectionwise hypothesis
5917	  equates the two middle terms \((f_i).\mathtt{app}\) and \((f_j).\mathtt{app}\) directly.
5918	  The only remaining identification is that the two \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation routes
5919	  --- built from the two down-set identities, one for \(U_i\) and one for \(U_j\) --- agree;
5920	  since both are parallel morphisms in the thin poset \((\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}}\),
5921	  they coincide by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). Hence the two restricted local sections
5922	  agree in \(\mathcal{H}(U_i \sqcap U_j)\), which is exactly \(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\).
5923	
5924	  Note the division of labour: the substantive content is the sectionwise agreement of the
5925	  \(f_i\), an equation in fixed abelian-group hom-types that a caller can supply; the
5926	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation and the \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) collapse of the
5927	  thin-poset routes are the only mechanical identifications, and they are legitimate
5928	  precisely because they act on the thin poset of opens of \(X\), never on the genuinely
5929	  distinct \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-hom-set section maps.
5930	
5931	  The other three sub-steps are mechanical: (b) gluing via
5932	  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
5933	  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g\) of the underlying presheaves of
5934	  \(M\) and \(N\) --- the
5935	  \(\top\)-section-to-morphism passage being the global-sections equivalence
5936	  \(\mathtt{presheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) / \(\mathtt{sheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) of the
5937	  hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) (which, since \(\top\) is terminal in \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
5938	  lands the glued section as an honest morphism of presheaves), \emph{not} a hand-unfold
5939	  of the \(\top\)-section; (c) the
5940	  sectionwise linearity check; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
5941	  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).
5942	
5943	  \emph{(ii) Promote to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear.} The linearity equation
5944	  \(g(r \cdot m) = r \cdot g(m)\) is a sectionwise identity between two sections of a
5945	  separated presheaf. It holds on each \(U_i\), because there \(g\) restricts to the
5946	  module morphism \(f_i\); since the two sides agree on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) and the
5947	  ambient presheaf is separated, they agree globally. Packaging \(g\) together with this
5948	  global linearity through the scheme-level wrapper \cref{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
5949	  (\(\mathtt{homMk}\)) yields a morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
5950	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); its restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\) and its
5951	  uniqueness are both inherited from the ab-sheaf amalgamation of step (i).
5952	
5953	  This gluing engine is substantially larger than a routine glue: it is dominated by the
5954	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality condition and the convert-and-recover bookkeeping, and
5955	  the naturality is the section-direction slice of
5956	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} --- so its true cost is bounded below by
5957	  building that sheaf-site equivalence, not by the surrounding assembly. It is therefore to be
5958	  scoped as a multi-piece build --- with \(\mathtt{localSection}\), together with its
5959	  naturality, built as a standalone verified lemma first --- rather than a one-shot.
5960	  The step computes no tensor stalk --- it is purely a statement about gluing morphisms
5961	  of sheaves --- and so it does not re-enter the stalk-tensor commutation.
5962	\end{proof}

5964	\begin{remark}
5965	[How the dual infrastructure discharges the \(\otimes\)-inverse]
5966	  \label{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}
5967	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial,
5968	        lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat, lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict,
5969	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
5970	  Once \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,def:presheaf_dual,%
5971	  lem:internal_hom_isSheaf,lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} are available,
5972	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is discharged with no further missing object:
5973	  take \(L^{-1} := \mathtt{dual}\,L\) (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}), which is a line
5974	  bundle by \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}. The global contraction \(\varepsilon_L
5975	  \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{not} obtained by sheafifying
5976	  the presheaf-level evaluation; it is instead built by \emph{gluing the canonical local
5977	  contraction morphisms}. On each trivialising open \(U\), the
5978	  restriction-compatibility iso \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} carries
5979	  \((L \otimes_X L^{-1})|_U\) to \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U\), and the
5980	  canonical local contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\)
5981	  of the free rank-one module with its dual is the left unitor
5982	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, an isomorphism. These local contractions are canonical,
5983	  hence agree on overlaps, and so glue --- by the A-bridge
5984	  \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat} --- to a single global morphism
5985	  \(\varepsilon_L \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\). Since ``being an
5986	  isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) restricts to an isomorphism on
5987	  each \(U\), it is a global isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim}
5988	  \mathcal{O}_X\) by the B-connector \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}.
5989	
5990	  This descent construction deliberately avoids the forbidden
5991	  ``sheafify-the-evaluation'' route --- which would re-enter the abandoned
5992	  tensor-stalk / sheafification-unit whiskering commutation --- and computes no tensor
5993	  stalk anywhere. It is the realization the ``infrastructure-blocked'' note in
5994	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} points to.
5995	\end{remark}

Two of the remaining sub-steps are mechanical: (b) gluing via
  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g\) of the underlying presheaves of
  \(M\) and \(N\) --- the
  \(\top\)-section-to-morphism passage being the global-sections equivalence
  \(\mathtt{presheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) / \(\mathtt{sheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) of the
  hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) (which, since \(\top\) is terminal in \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
  lands the glued section as an honest morphism of presheaves), \emph{not} a hand-unfold
  of the \(\top\)-section; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).

  Sub-step (c) --- the sectionwise \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linearity check that promotes the
  glued morphism \(g\) of underlying ab-sheaves to a morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
  --- is, by contrast, \emph{not} mechanical. It is the point at which the several module
  structures in play (the native section-ring action of \(\mathcal{O}_X\), and the
  restriction-of-scalars action induced by the open immersions) must be reconciled. The
  required identity \(g(r \cdot x) = r \cdot g(x)\), checked sectionwise over an open
  \(P\), splits into three legs.
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{(c.i) The \(M\)-leg (source semilinearity).} Restriction of a section
      against a structure-ring scalar is discharged by the native, Γ-module-level
      functoriality identity \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.map\_smul}\,M\): for a restriction
      map \(i\) one has
      \(M(i)(r \cdot x) = X(i)(r) \cdot M(i)(x)\) --- an equation phrased directly over the
      section ring \(\mathcal{O}_X\), with \emph{no} restriction-of-scalars artifact. This
      leg closes cleanly.
    \item \emph{(c.ii) The \(f\)-leg (the genuine obstacle).} The section component
      \((f_i).\mathtt{app}(P)\) is, by construction, linear over the
      \emph{restriction-of-scalars} action carried by \((M|_{\iota_i})(P)\) --- restriction
      of scalars along the structure-ring map of the open immersion \(\iota_i\) --- whereas
      the \(f\)-leg of the goal feeds in the \emph{native}
      \(\mathcal{O}_X(\mathrm{image})\)-module action. These two actions are
      propositionally equal but \emph{not} definitionally equal. The reconciliation is the
      genuine content of (c): for an open immersion the structure-ring comparison
      isomorphism is the identity --- \((U_i).\iota.\mathtt{appIso} = \mathtt{Iso.refl}\)
      --- so the ring map along which scalars are restricted is the identity \(\mathbf{1}\);
      and restriction of scalars along the \emph{identity} ring map returns the original
      action,
      \(c \cdot_{\,\mathrm{restr}\,\mathbf{1}} x = \mathbf{1}(c) \cdot x = c \cdot x\),
      identified by \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars.smul\_def}\) together with
      \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsId'App}\). This identity-ring-map smul bridge --- the
      localization observation that restriction of scalars along an isomorphism that
      happens to be the identity reproduces the native module action --- is the substantive
      mathematics of sub-step (c).
    \item \emph{(c.iii) The \(N\)-leg (target semilinearity).} Once the bridge of (c.ii) is
      in place, the target leg is discharged by the native identity
      \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.map\_smul}\,N\), exactly as in (c.i); the scalars transported

      arising from the down-set identity \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(W)\bigr) = W\) compose
      to the identity on the overlap.
  \end{itemize}

  and (d) …" sentence previously lumped (c) in with the mechanical steps. Split it:

    identity-ring-map identification of module actions):
    - **(c.i)** M-leg semilinearity via the native Γ-module-level functoriality identity

      restriction along the identity ring map returns the native action

      `ModuleCat.restrictScalars.smul_def` + `restrictScalarsId'App`. Named as the genuine
      content of (c).

      transported scalars reconcile because the two open-set `eqToHom` maps from
